macro_segments.title: Resolution of the Bikkurim Debate Between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish Regarding Non-Ideal Growing Conditions

micro_segments.title: Opening Summary: The Dispute Between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish Over Bikkurim from Non-Ideal Locations
The Gemara Resolves the Contradiction with 'Tannai Hi' — It Is a Tannaitic Dispute
Braita Distinguishing Cases: When Bikkurim from Rooftops Is Valid vs. Completely Forbidden
Conclusion: Bikkurim Must Represent Choice Produce, Contrasting with the Leniency for Menachot Flour

macro_segments.title: Aggadic Digression: The Story of Yochna and Mamre Challenging Moshe — 'Bringing Straw to Ofrayim'

micro_segments.title: Context: The Mishna Mentions Afurayim as Second-Grade Wheat Source, Prompting an Aggadic Aside
Yochna and Mamre Tell Moshe: 'You Are Bringing Straw to Ofrayim' — Coals to Newcastle
Moshe's Response: In the City of Vegetables, the Best Vegetables Rise to the Top

macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Non-Ideal Grain for Menachot: Manured Fields, Irrigated Fields, and the Rotating of Crops

micro_segments.title: Mishna: You May Not Bring Flour from Manured Fields, Irrigated Fields, or Fields with Trees — But Bedi'avad It Is Valid
Discussion of Rashi vs. Tosafot: Does the Mishna Apply Only to the Omer and Shtei HaLechem or to All Menachot?
The Mishna's Prescription for Ideal Grain: Overturning the Soil the First Year and Planting Seventy Days Before Pesach
The Gizbar Tests the Flour by Inserting His Hand — If Dust Rises It Is Pasul Until Resifted; Wormy Flour Is Pasul Outright
Gemara's Question: Does the Mishna Mean to Overturn the Soil Once or Twice Before Planting?
Braita Cited: Southern Fields Exposed to Full Sun, Overturning Soil in Year One Then Plowing Twice in Year Two
Description of the Ideal Yield: Stalk One Zeret, Ear Two Zeretayim — An Extraordinary High Ratio
The Full Production Process: Harvesting, Threshing, Winnowing, Grinding, Sifting, and Presenting to the Gizbar
Gemara Resolves Mishna vs. Braita Contradiction on Double Plowing via 'Kan Ba-avoda, Kan B'she-eina Avoda'
Discussion of Shmitta: Could One Plow Without Planting in a Shmitta Year, and Under What Justification?
Rabbi Yochanan's Statement: The Omer Comes Only from Southern Fields Exposed to Full Sunlight
Abba Shaul: The Omer Came from the Valley of Beit Makle — A Three-Se'ah Southern Field with Alternating Rotation
Rav Hilkiya bar Tuvi's Corner Field: He Practiced the Rotation and Sold the Double-Yield Wheat for Menachot

macro_segments.title: Laws of Wormy Flour and Wood: The Question of Majority, and Whether Sanctifying Them Incurs Lashes Like Ba'al Mum

micro_segments.title: Braita: Flour That Is Majority Wormy Is Invalid; So Too Wheat That Is Majority Wormy
Rabbi Yirmiya's Question: Is the Majority Measured Per Individual Grain or Across the Entire Se'ah?
Rava's Question: If One Sanctifies Wormy Grain, Does He Receive Lashes as for Ba'al Mum? — Teiku
The Same Question Applied to Wormy Wood Donated for the Altar — Shmuel: Dry Wood Can Be Scraped Clean
Rava's Parallel Question for Wood: Does Sanctifying Wormy Wood Incur Lashes for Ba'al Mum? — Teiku

macro_segments.title: The Mishna and Gemara on Oil for Menachot: Ideal Sources, Disqualifying Conditions, and Aggadic Stories

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Tekoa Is the Premier Source of Olive Oil; Regev in Ever HaYarden Is Second; All Lands Are Technically Valid But These Are Preferred
Discussion: Can the Oil for the Omer Come from Ever HaYarden or Even from Chutz La'aretz?
Mishna Continues: Prohibited Oil Sources — Manured or Irrigated Groves, Grain-Interspersed Groves, Unripe Olives, Soaked, Pickled, or Boiled Olives
Gemara Aggada: Why Did Yoav Go Specifically to Tekoa for a Wise Woman? — Olive Oil Sharpens the Mind
The Verse 'Vetoveil Bashemen Raglo' and the Portion of Asher — Oil Flowed Like a Wellspring
The Story of the Officer from Ludkaya Who Sought a Million Maneh of Oil and Found It with One Humble Farmer in Gush Chalav
Conclusion of the Story: The Farmer Had Even More Oil Than Needed — Fulfilling 'Mitroshesh VeHon Rav'
